stem: The function \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[b]])}{\left(z - ([[p]])\right)\left(z - ([[q]])\right)} \) has a simple pole at \( z = [[p]] \). Compute \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f(z) = \lim_{z \to [[p]]} \left(z - ([[p]])\right) f(z) \).
solution: For a simple pole at \( z = [[p]] \), \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f = \lim_{z\to [[p]]} (z-[[p]])f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])([[p]]) + ([[b]])}{([[p]]) - ([[q]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the residue at the other pole \( z = [[q]] \), not at \( z = [[p]] \).
This flips the sign of the denominator \( (z-p) \), giving the negative of the residue.
This forgets to divide by \( (p - q) \) after cancelling the pole factor.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by two from the correct residue.
This doubles the correct residue.

stem: The function \( g(z) = \dfrac{z^{2} + ([[b]])z + ([[c]])}{z - ([[p]])} \) has a simple pole at \( z = [[p]] \), where the denominator vanishes to first order. Compute \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} g(z) \).
solution: Since the pole factor \( (z-[[p]]) \) already appears alone in the denominator, \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} g = \lim_{z\to [[p]]}(z-[[p]])g(z) \) reduces to plugging \( z=[[p]] \) directly into the numerator: \( ([[p]])^2 + ([[b]])([[p]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The residue is not automatically \( 0 \); it equals the numerator evaluated at the pole, which need not vanish.
This has the wrong sign; the residue is the numerator's value at \( z=[[p]] \), not its negative.
This is the derivative of the numerator at \( z=[[p]] \), not the numerator's value there.
This drops the linear term \( [[b]]z \) of the numerator when evaluating at \( z=[[p]] \).
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This doubles the correct residue.

stem: The function \( h(z) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{(z - ([[p]]))(z - ([[q]]))(z - ([[r]]))} \) has simple poles at \( z = [[p]], [[q]], [[r]] \). Compute \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} h(z) \), the residue at \( z = [[p]] \) only.
solution: Cancelling the \( (z-[[p]]) \) factor and setting \( z=[[p]] \) in what remains, \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} h = \dfrac{[[c]]}{(([[p]])-([[q]]))(([[p]])-([[r]]))} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the residue at \( z=[[q]] \), not at \( z=[[p]] \).
This is the residue at \( z=[[r]] \), not at \( z=[[p]] \).
This only cancels one of the two remaining pole factors, forgetting \( (z-[[r]]) \) in the denominator.
This only cancels one of the two remaining pole factors, forgetting \( (z-[[q]]) \) in the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This doubles the correct residue.

stem: The function \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[d]])}{\left(z - ([[p]])\right)\left(z^{2} + ([[b]])z + ([[c]])\right)} \) has a real simple pole at \( z = [[p]] \); the quadratic factor \( z^{2}+([[b]])z+([[c]]) \) has no real roots (its two complex zeros are not our concern here). Compute \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f(z) \).
solution: Cancelling \( (z-[[p]]) \) and evaluating the rest at \( z=[[p]] \), \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f = \dfrac{([[a]])([[p]]) + ([[d]])}{([[p]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[p]]) + ([[c]])} = [[answer]] \); the irreducible quadratic factor is simply evaluated at the pole, not solved.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by the quadratic factor evaluated at \( z=[[p]] \).
This uses only the linear term \( [[b]] \) of the quadratic instead of evaluating the full quadratic at \( z=[[p]] \).
This uses only the constant term \( [[c]] \) of the quadratic instead of evaluating the full quadratic at \( z=[[p]] \).
This has the wrong sign on the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This doubles the correct residue.
